title: Evangelion A Boys Own Odyssey
wikitext:
[]: Evangelion: A Boys Own Odyssey             Quote Post by Sheldon Danielson » Tue Nov 17, 2009 12:30 pm When I first watched Evangelion last winter I was blown away, like many others. My response was to write about it… and think about it… and write about it… etc. over many cycles. I gradually evolved an overall interpretation that sees Evangelion not as a drama of combat with strange beings, but rather as the internal story of a 14-year old boy’s epic struggle to overcome the fallout from a traumatic childhood. The result is on the Web at http://www.kanedatetsuo.com . (~21,400 words) In forming my interpretation I have included material from sources such as: the Epic of Gilgamesh , the Iliad , the Odyssey , the Bible, Pilgrim’s Progress , Plato, the legendary trickster, and others. My paper rests on two “theories,” one straightforward, the other more radical. It’s my view that interpretations of art are necessarily personal and depend on who we are and what our experiences have been, so I make absolutely no claim to being “right” or “final” or “authoritative” or anything of the sort. The purpose Web publishing, as I see it, is to share our interpretations – however personal – in the hope that we might add to the richness of the world and broaden our view of life itself, especially as experienced by others. I invite EVA fans to look at my interpretation and post your comments or questions. I will be here to respond.

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Tue Nov 17, 2009 1:38 pm Well, your essay is certainly comprehensive, if a little speculative. It's certainly an interesting literary analysis; I don't think I've seen Evangelion paralleled with so many works at once before. (Complete with references too.) The work is large, and the community is used to dealing with more digestible portions. However, hopefully we can rise to the occasion. Before we begin however, could you give a short summary of the ideas and topics within the essay, and in particular highlight those portions you would most like to discuss. Clearly, there is much speculation here. However, there are many insights too. Hopefully everybody can have a productive and constructive discussion on the pros and cons of the many points raised in the essay.

[]: Evangelion: A Boy's Own Odyssey             Quote Post by Sheldon Danielson » Tue Nov 17, 2009 2:42 pm Thanks for your thoughtful - and fast - reply. I’m sure you can understand my reluctance to simplify a long account down to a few bullet points. My greatest fear is that people will simply respond to those points (“Nonsense! Ridiculous!”) without reading the essay. They might say the same things after reading it, but at least they would know why I said what I did. That’s part of what takes so long. Another part is that Evangelion did not come into being out of cultural nothingness. Its makers work in, and are deeply aware of, world culture going back to the Epic of Gilgamesh, our first known epic. We and they live in a world created by our past. I try to show how acknowledging the past can deepen our appreciation of the products of our own culture. This is one of the goals of Humanities courses in college. Evangelion is fully deserving of a college-level treatment, in my opinion. I have faith that there will be at least a few people who are both fascinated by Evangelion and would have fun reading a long account of it. There are some suggested shortcuts at the top of the paper. Once again: The interpretation is mine and I take responsibility for it. It is not intended to replace or negate anyone else’s interpretation.

[]:             Quote Post by Xard » Tue Nov 17, 2009 3:29 pm Hello, I promise to read your writing, it sounds most interesting :) I recommend you read Campbell's The Hero With a Thousand Faces, some of Jung's writings on archetypes and about monomythic format in general. This is to say: NGE and Odyssey share a lot material due to their archetypal content. There's Hero's Journey et al involved in both works. Odyssey might be de facto example of that archetype

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Nov 17, 2009 4:37 pm I will definitely make it a point to read this (I bookmarked it). The only problem I tend to have with intertextual analyses is that it too often boils down to narrative archetypes that are present in an extremely large amount of narrative art; especially when we're dealing with works like The Odyssey and Gilgamesh. Plus, there's the whole problem of NGE being a visual narrative and not a literary narrative; even if it is very literary in much of its execution, it draws as much from cinematic history as it does from literary history and I tend to think the former is more important because Anno is a stronger director/auteur than he is a writer. With that said, I still think this looks quite interesting. Props just for being able to write something on NGE that's 20k words long. I imagine I've written about 200x that much in the last three years I've been into it but I'm awful at putting my thoughts into these kind of formal essays; which is probably why I spend so much time on discussion forums.

[]:             Quote Post by Sheldon Danielson » Tue Nov 17, 2009 5:33 pm Quote: Plus, there's the whole problem of NGE being a visual narrative and not a literary narrative. That is certainly correct, and I admit that I approached NGE as a literary work. I would be a little out of my depth in discussing it as a cinematic work, so I did not try. I'll have to leave that to others.

[]:             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Tue Nov 17, 2009 7:16 pm Both myself and Xard have written quite a bit on NGE's cinematic technique but I'd be interested in seeing how much this overlaps with the literary elements. Classic film took much of its cues from literature, obviously, yet the narrative techniques of the camera - especially that directors like Hitchcock, Welles, and Ford developed - and how they relate to analogous literary techniques is something I've never seen written about (though, granted, I haven't looked much). I've often found it difficult in resolving how NGE can be so full of literary tradition (I've especially written about Anno's use of allusions) and yet being incredibly cinematic and how these two things effect each other... It's probably something I would write about in an essay if, ya know, I did that sort of thing.

[]:             Quote Post by Merridian » Tue Nov 17, 2009 9:37 pm @Sheldon Danielson I’ve read your paper and have a bunch of comments/critiques. I tried to keep it succinct, but… well… I’ve spoiler-ed it for size & content, since it’s tl;dr as fuck and I don’t wanna turn this into a certain other thread on this site . Oh who am I kidding? It’ll probably morph into something like that anyway… SPOILER: Show

[]: Shinji-1, Shinji-2             Quote Post by Sheldon Danielson » Tue Nov 17, 2009 11:30 pm I very much appreciate your comments and willingness to engage. The two Shinjis are certainly one of the most “out there” aspects of my interpretation. Here is Shinji near the end of EoE: “I only felt pain when I existed in that reality. So I thought it was alright to run away. But there was nothing good in the place I escaped to either, because I didn’t exist there, and so no one existed.” “Because I didn’t exist there, and so one existed.” This says to me that everything we have been watching has been in Shinji’s head and has no reality. Shinji-2 is the Shinji we have been watching – the imaginary one. But if there is a fantasy, there has to be (at least in principle) a fantasizer. He is my Shinji-1. They are very nearly the same, except that one does not exist. But somebody had to have those fantasies, and for convenience I call him Shinji-1. Shinji-1 is part of my invented back-story, whose object is just to provide an interpretive framework. You can certainly take it or leave it. Another reason for my Shinji-1 is to avoid speculating about Anno-san’s history or frame of mind. I'm not into long-distance psychoanalyzing. I have intentionally not said anything about Japanese sociology because I am not qualified to do so. This is the first forum I have ever participated in. If any of my posts are blank, or upside down, or look like Sanskrit, just chalk it up to inexperience.

[]: Re: Shinji-1, Shinji-2             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Wed Nov 18, 2009 4:25 pm Sheldon Danielson wrote: Shinji-1 is part of my invented back-story, whose object is just to provide an interpretive framework. You can certainly take it or leave it. Another reason for my Shinji-1 is to avoid speculating about Anno-san’s history or frame of mind. I'm not into long-distance psychoanalyzing

[]: Is Shinji-1 Anno?             Quote Post by Sheldon Danielson » Wed Nov 18, 2009 7:39 pm Boy, you really know how to put a poor guy in the hot seat. Of course what you say makes sense (and I guess everybody believes it), but as a matter of professional courtesy (or whatever) I just can't bring myself to say it. I'm a fiction writer too, and I might not like it if people identified me with my fictional characters. On the other hand, it is a well-known occupational hazard and anyone in the business should be prepared for it. But maybe we can come to a resonable compromise - I know your heart is in the right place. As a matter of protocol, I will continue to refer to Shinji-1, but others can call him anything they like. The important thing is that there is someone back there, who suffered, and who worked on his problems by creating great art. Can you buy that? Edit: On further thought, I'll try to be more responsive. I've been in exactly the uncomfortable position I've described. That probably contributes to my caution in the case of Anno. But I also believe that the class snapshot near the end of EoE is important, showing us the fantasizer as Anno wants us to see him - recovered, joyful, and back in the real world. He certainly had something in mind, and that is my reading of it. I don't doubt for a moment that Anno saw himself in that photo.

[]: Re: Is Shinji-1 Anno?             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Thu Nov 19, 2009 9:46 am Sheldon Danielson wrote: The important thing is that there is someone back there, who suffered, and who worked on his problems by creating great art. Can you buy that?

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Thu Nov 19, 2009 10:46 am The Asuka-Scylla comparison seems rather odd. There's quite some stuff you've put up here and there that's worthy of thought. I agree with OMF: you seem to have hit the nail on the head with Toji. And as for Asuka being the violence and brutality that Shinji rejects, I disagree. The girl has had an absolutely black past, and she has simply forgotten to regard herself as a human being. Maybe the only reason why Shinji and Misato are the protagonists in this story is that they regard themselves as human in some way that the others don't? I think the background behind a plausible Shinji-1 is heavily speculative, and makes him seem a little like a Gary Stu. There are many roads to Shinji; and the Shinji that we see in the series has gone down one of the most painful ones. By the way, Kaworu is not standing on the statue of a Gryphon- he's standing on what used to be the neck of an Angel statue, although what the statue is doing out in the middle of the crater lake is anyone's guess.

[]:             Quote Post by Sharaz Destler » Thu Nov 19, 2009 11:21 am UrsusArctos wrote: By the way, Kaworu is not standing on the statue of a Gryphon- he's standing on what used to be the neck of an Angel statue, although what the statue is doing out in the middle of the crater lake is anyone's guess.

[]: Many issues, many characters             Quote Post by Sheldon Danielson » Thu Nov 19, 2009 5:54 pm The marvelous Toji is an example of how – in the right hands – less can be more. Anyone who thinks that anime is “just cartoons” should study Toji. Or, for that matter, nearly any part of NGE. I think about NGE from a storytellers’ perspective. The following very interesting paper considers Akira from an anthropological perspective. “ Akira , Postmodernism and Resistance.” This is in the book: The Worlds of Japanese Popular Culture , edited by D. P. Martinez. Characters who are only symbols tend to be boring. If the Children were no more than chips off the ol’ Shinji, and nothing more, they would be boring; if they were otherwise empty vessels for Shinji’s fragmented personality, they would be boring. Instead, they are given histories and personalities that deepen our experience of them, making them sympathetic and fully rounded. Asuka’s black past is part of why we care about her; the same for Rei. Rei means instance or example (among others). Good name for a clone! The exception is Kaworu, who mostly is a symbol, but a very complicated and multilayered one. Imagine meeting a saint: might be a bit intimidating. Yet even he (partly through the excellent voice acting of Ishida Akira), has an identifiably human personality; someone we might (or might not) like to meet. What I try to do is carry all these conflicting ideas in my head at once, so that the result is a “superposition state” of different realities. (Quantum superposition is explained – amazingly – in the anime series Noein . Wikipedia, too, of course.) I totally agree with UrsusArctos: “there are many roads to Shinji.” Again, Noein makes this explicit. The first time I saw EoE I did not understand the final scene, and didn’t like it. Fortunately, it’s at the end, so I did a lot of writing before coming to that point. When I came up with my “fragmented Shinji” theory, the final scene suddenly jumped into focus. Now I think it is the most beautiful and dramatically economical scene in the whole saga. What is the congratulations scene? Doesn’t ring a bell.

[]: Re: Many issues, many characters             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Thu Nov 19, 2009 6:20 pm Sheldon Danielson wrote: What is the congratulations scene? Doesn’t ring a bell.

[]: Congratulations scene             Quote Post by Sheldon Danielson » Thu Nov 19, 2009 6:33 pm Ah, yes, I remember now. Yes, I've seen it, but I prefer EoE. I think that by EoE Anno had fully decided what he wanted to say, so I take the name End of Evangelion seriously. I did like the original 25 and 26, but I like EoE even more. I take the congratulations much the same way: as an acknowledgment of Shinji's growth. Here's another name / meaning correspondence: Shinji is the stem of the verb believe or trust . Was this intended? Hard to think it wasn't, though it's not quite so convincing as Rei.

[]:             Quote Post by UrsusArctos » Thu Nov 19, 2009 8:00 pm Sharaz Destler wrote: Now, when you say Angel, do you mean an angel as in "a white-robed tinkerbell with halo, wings, and lyre" or do you mean an angel as in "that which invades Tokyo-3?" I assume you mean the former, which would indicate that the crater-lake might have been a park or graveyard (or, more chillingly, BOTH) at some point pre-Second Impact.

[]: Re: Many issues, many characters             Quote Post by Eva Yojimbo » Thu Nov 19, 2009 11:28 pm Sheldon Danielson wrote: The first time I saw EoE I did not understand the final scene, and didn’t like it. Fortunately, it’s at the end, so I did a lot of writing before coming to that point. When I came up with my “fragmented Shinji” theory, the final scene suddenly jumped into focus. Now I think it is the most beautiful and dramatically economical scene in the whole saga.

[]: Re: Congratulations scene             Quote Post by Ornette » Fri Nov 20, 2009 12:59 am Sheldon Danielson wrote: Here's another name / meaning correspondence: Shinji is the stem of the verb believe or trust . Was this intended? Hard to think it wasn't, though it's not quite so convincing as Rei.

[]: Congratulations scene             Quote Post by Sheldon Danielson » Thu Nov 19, 2009 6:33 pm Ah, yes, I remember now. Yes, I've seen it, but I prefer EoE. I think that by EoE Anno had fully decided what he wanted to say, so I take the name End of Evangelion seriously. I did like the original 25 and 26, but I like EoE even more. I take the congratulations much the same way: as an acknowledgment of Shinji's growth. Here's another name / meaning correspondence: Shinji is the stem of the verb believe or trust . Was this intended? Hard to think it wasn't, though it's not quite so convincing as Rei.
